coredata.dc:description: This paper presents the development of a combined lifetime model used to estimate the capacity fade evolution and internal resistance increase of 43 Ah big capacity nickel-manganese-cobalt oxide (NMC) cell playing the leading role in the automotive industry. However, these NMC based lithium-ion cells passed through capacity fade uncertainty problems during their cycling and calendaring periods. Therefore, to investigate the nonlinear aging behavior of the NMC cell, an extensive lifetime characterization of the cell was performed with an efficient testing methodology accompanied by large and quality datasets collected for more than two years. According to the analysis result of the experimental dataset, the cycling capacity fade was strongly affected by the depth of discharge (DoD), temperature, C-rate, and slightly affected by middle state of charge (Mid-SoC). On the other hand, the calendar capacity fade was affected by high storage state of charge (SoC), and high temperature. In the previous studies, limitations related to complexity, accuracy, and computational difficulties of aging models are observed. Therefore, to compromise these challenges, in this paper, a semi-empirical fitted equivalent circuit model-based lifetime model is proposed. The validation of the developed model is performed by using a highly dynamic Worldwide Harmonized Light Vehicles Test (WLTC) profile and a root mean square error (RMSE) of 2% was found. In this study, new knowledge is gained with the developed lifetime model which benefits car manufacturers using big capacity NMC-based cells and hence can utilize the model to estimate the available capacity and lifetime of the battery cells.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper presents the development of a combined lifetime model used to estimate the capacity fade evolution and internal resistance increase of 43 Ah big capacity nickel-manganese-cobalt oxide (NMC) cell playing the leading role in the automotive industry. However, these NMC based lithium-ion cells passed through capacity fade uncertainty problems during their cycling and calendaring periods. Therefore, to investigate the nonlinear aging behavior of the NMC cell, an extensive lifetime characterization of the cell was performed with an efficient testing methodology accompanied by large and quality datasets collected for more than two years. According to the analysis result of the experimental dataset, the cycling capacity fade was strongly affected by the depth of discharge (DoD), temperature, C-rate, and slightly affected by middle state of charge (Mid-SoC). On the other hand, the calendar capacity fade was affected by high storage state of charge (SoC), and high temperature. In the previous studies, limitations related to complexity, accuracy, and computational difficulties of aging models are observed. Therefore, to compromise these challenges, in this paper, a semi-empirical fitted equivalent circuit model-based lifetime model is proposed. The validation of the developed model is performed by using a highly dynamic Worldwide Harmonized Light Vehicles Test (WLTC) profile and a root mean square error (RMSE) of 2% was found. In this study, new knowledge is gained with the developed lifetime model which benefits car manufacturers using big capacity NMC-based cells and hence can utilize the model to estimate the available capacity and lifetime of the battery cells.

ce:section.ce:para: In the electric vehicle (EV) application area, lithium-ion battery technologies are crucial in storing and supplying the required energy [1,2]. In addition to the use of these batteries in automotive services, it becomes common practice to be used in different stationary application areas [3,4]. Though different options of battery storage technologies are available, the nickel-manganese-cobalt oxide (NMC) chemistry-based cells took the lead for being utilized in vehicular services. This is because compared to other battery types, these batteries retain better power and gravimetric/volumetric energy capabilities [5]. Nevertheless, when these batteries are subjected to different cycling operating conditions, their performance degraded over time and results in capacity fade uncertainty problems [6–8]. In addition to the cycling aging effect, the capacity loss and internal resistance increase also occurs during calendar aging [9,10] resulted from the chemical nature of lithium-ion cells. The evolution of a solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) [11,12] and lithium plating [13–15] are among the electrochemical effects responsible for capacity degradation. Besides the electrochemical effect, there are also stress factors on which the capacity degradation of the cells depends. These are the state of charge (SoC), temperature, depth of discharge (DoD), and current-rate (C-rate) [11,16,17].

The aging of lithium-ion battery is a complicated aspect with enormous electrochemical processes subjected to different operational circumstances which gradually resulted in both cycling and calendar aging capacity loss effects. When batteries are passed through calendar aging, capacity degradation is resulted which is commonly caused by storage SoC and temperature stress factors [18–22]. So far, for calendar aging prediction of lithium-ion batteries, different types of modeling techniques have been implemented [23,24]. In addition, the capacity fade results due to cycling aging are investigated by different authors which are mostly influenced by DoD, C-rate, and temperature factors [25,26]. The resulting cycling and calendar aging effects provides a capacity loss and Ri increase of the battery cell [27–30], which gradually decreases the battery performance. Therefore, to assure the efficient performance and long-term usability of lithium-ion battery cells, it is important to conduct extensive aging characterization tests accompanied by an appropriate aging model which enables to understand the battery degradation behavior [31–34]. For precise determination of the remaining useful life and aging rate of the cells, an accurate lifetime model parametrized with lifetime test results is crucial [18,31,35–37]. In addition to the lifetime investigations, the thermal and electrical performance evaluations of lithium-ion battery packs controlled by suitable battery management system (BMS) are also found to be vital in electric vehicle applications [38–41].

So far, different authors have anticipated various types of methodologies for the development of a lifetime model applied for predicting the capacity fade of the battery cells. The electrochemical, mathematical, electric equivalent circuit and statistical models are among the types of methodologies used. In addition, the semi-empirical and artificial intelligence (AI) based models has been developed for the estimation of the remaining useful life (RUL) and state of health (SoH) of battery cells [18,23,42,43]. However, the use of AI techniques suffers from the prediction of the cells aging behaviors with computational power requirement and limited boundary conditions. For the case of the electrochemical model, though it works with real internal chemical and electrochemical reactions of battery materials and studies the degradation effects, it suffers from the complexity and high computational cost requirements [44–47]. This makes the electrochemical model difficult and not cost-effective for deploying it. The mathematical model type requires mathematical tools for the representation of each component and neural network, fuzzy logic, and particle filtering mechanisms were used in combination for the calculation of respective responses [48–50]. These techniques are found to be complex and demand high computational power [51]. Data-driven approaches are also the other methods with fast computational speeds, however, these methods have drawback of providing the degradation effects in a limited boundary circumstance [52–56]. While dealing with prognosis analysis of batteries, statistical models are used which works by learning and gathering information from experimental test results [11,57,58], but these methods are not flexible and inefficient due to the limitation to operate with very specific application scenario. Besides, the equivalent circuit model (ECM) is also another modeling technique which follows performance-based modeling approach consisted up of the electrical elements for determination of the battery responses [59–63].

Researchers have performed the aging analysis and modeling of lithium-ion battery cells focusing on the capacity degradation due to cycling and or calendaring effects separately by neglecting their simultaneous impact of internal resistance increase in relation to SoH [9,21,23,33,36,50]. The previous aging studies did not consider the impact of the base-loss effect on the estimation of capacity fade evolution by the developed model. In addition, most of the above lifetime investigations were focused on low nominal capacity cells (mostly less or equal to 20 Ah), and validation of the models were also performed with a limited cycle number of dynamic profiles [22,26], and short range of impact factors.

Therefore, to alleviate the beforementioned gaps, and hence provide a compromise between complexity, accuracy, and computational difficulties, in this paper, a semi-empirical fitted ECM based combined lifetime model is proposed. The model is named as combined because, the cycling and calendar lifetime models are jointly implemented. The developed lifetime model is normally coupled with dual-polarization equivalent circuit (DP-ECM) electro-thermal model. In this study, an extended aging condition followed by huge and quality dataset of cycling and calendar aging results were used for parameterization and development of a robust lifetime model applied for estimation of the capacity fade and internal resistance (Ri) increase of a large 43 Ah cell. In this paper, new contributions are made where the proposed lifetime model considers the base-loss effect to take the initial capacity evolution contribution into account. Moreover, to adjust the capacity degradation in relation to the aging factors, their influence relationships are implemented in the model mathematically, and hence the developed tool for the lifetime model preparation can be taken as a base work towards online implementation. Finally, a dynamic Worldwide Harmonized Light Vehicle Test (WLTC) profile with prolonged number of cycles is used for validation of the lifetime model and a promising result with root mean square error (RMSE) of 2% accuracy were found.

The rest of the manuscript sections are presented as follows: Section 2 discussed experimental setup and overview of the NMC cell. Section 3 enlightens the experimental results and the developed lifetime model. Section 4 discussed the validation of the lifetime model. Section 5 presented the conclusions and future work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental setup and NMC cell description
ce:section.ce:para: This section discussed the procedures used for the collection of experimental data of the battery capacity degradation tested under various cycling and calendar aging conditions. The cell used for experimenting is a large format prismatic cell consisted up of NMC/C based cathode and graphite based anode. Lithium salt or lithium hexafluorophosphate is used as an electrolyte material for these cells and has a nominal capacity of 43 Ah, nominal voltage of 3.6 V, 840 g weight, and high power density of 1200 W/kg which makes the cell suitable to be used in automotive applications. In addition, the operating voltage region of the cell is 3 to 4.2 V with internal resistance of ≤2 mΩ and dimension of 27.5 mm ∗ 148 mm ∗ 91 mm. PEC manufactured testers and CTS customed climate chambers for ambient temperature control were used during the test campaign.

The cycling and calendar aging testing procedure was presented according to the testing conditions summarized in Tables 1 and 2
respectively. To investigate the decisive factors of aging of cells, a refined test matrix is used, and the capacity fade rate of the cells influenced by different stress factors has been evaluated. For quantification of these influences, a large batch of measured data resulted from extensive test conditions of cycling aging is used. For determining the influence of the DoD on capacity degradation, various combinations of cycling conditions with similar mid-SoC, temperature, and the charge-discharge rate is used, where this technique is valid for all influencing factors. In addition, the storage SoC and temperature are considered as the factors influencing calendar aging. During calendar aging, for each combination of storage temperature (10 °C, 25 °C, 45 °C) and storage SoC (100%, 20%), one cell was used with a total of six cells completely tested. The measurement of capacity fade and Ri increase was performed every 28 days.

The lifetime testing methodology used in this research was aimed to develop a precise lifetime model used for identification of the battery degradation behavior and estimation of capacity fade evolution during the cell's lifetime. The development process of battery modeling consists of a series of aging tests at given standard testing procedures used in the automotive industry for capturing the lifetime behavior efficiently.

The phenomena of cycling aging occur when the cell passes through the charging or discharging process. The procedure used for the cycling aging test focused on the finding of the correlation between the fundamental aging processes in the battery cell in line with the driving conditions under a real application scenario. The cycling aging profile considered for one of the many conditions is already presented in a separate supplementary document.

During the lifetime of a cell, the methodology of lifetime testing is designed in such a way that it can measure and provide a huge amount of information on the cell capacity. To understand the aging impacts, two types of capacity check-up protocols are performed: Short check-up (SCU) and detailed check-up (DCU). The first one is done in every 100 FEC and the later one in every 300 FEC. The capacity measurement tests are performed according to Table 1 for which complete charge and discharge at 0.5C are carried out. In addition to the useful capacity extraction, the cell internal resistance was estimated during each capacity measurement test using the Hybrid Pulse Power Characterization (HPPC) test procedure. The test consists of applying a pulse at three SoC points of 80%, 50%, and 20%, with three different C-rates (0.33C, 0.5C, and 1C). The resistance increase is also calculated from the actual and the nominal value, where the resistance estimated at 50% SoC and C/2 discharge pulse is considered as a reference value. When the resistance value reaches 200%, the cell lifetime is considered completely used.

The 43 Ah NMC based lithium-ion cell passes through extensive degradation tests of cycling and calendar aging tests. The lifetime characterization test was performed for each cell according to the short and detailed lifetime check-up conditions stated in Section 2.2. The summary of the test procedures is presented in Fig. 1 .

While conducting the lifetime characterization tests, a set of reference performance tests (RPTs) have been conducted, where the capacity, OCV, and HPPC tests are among the tests used to follow up the degradation status during no-load and loaded conditions.

A total of 40 cells were completely tested for investigating the cycling aging. The battery cells have been cycled with constant charge and discharge profile and at specific mid-SoC, C-rate, DoD, FEC and temperature ranges. The capacity fade result has been counted at each of 100 FEC and parameter values are updated until the end of life (EoL) criteria are met. Relative capacity degradation (RCD) of 20% compared to beginning of life (BoL) capacity value of the cell is taken as ending criteria of the cell life. Capacity loss due to the calendar aging effect was also performed by using different combinations of resting conditions. Reference check-up tests were performed on each cell by changing the cell to a predefined storage SoC and stored at a defined temperature and then capacity fade and internal resistance values are estimated.

For validation of the lifetime model and evaluate its accuracy, additional cycling tests were performed by using a dynamic WLTC profile [64]. The single WLTC cycle consisted up of two urban plus two suburban sets (900 s each) making it an hour-long profile. WLTC cycling tests have been performed at an initial SoC of 90% and two different scenarios – at 25 °C and 45 °C temperature. During validation cycling, short RPTs have been conducted at consistent intervals used to follow up the capacity loss during aging as per the real conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental results and lifetime model development
ce:section.ce:para: The lifetime characterization tests were performed for identification of the battery cell capacity degradation behavior resulted from both cycling and calendar aging effects. The cycling aging effect was mainly investigated by considering the mid-SoC, C-rate, DoD, and temperature parameters [18,52], whereas calendaring effects were limited to temperature and storage SoC parameters [65], where in this study the impact of storage time is also considered. For estimating the actual value of cycling capacity fade, the capacity degradation due to calendar aging has been subtracted from the total capacity fade results. The resulting accurate capacity degradation analysis output is very important for the development and parameterization of the lifetime model. The detail analysis of the cycling and calendar capacity fade results are presented as follows.

In addition to the common stress factors which influence capacity degradation, there are various electrochemical phenomena including active and non-active material losses inside the cell resulted in the degradation of capacity [33,46,66–69]. Acquiring a clear understanding of these factors and parameters influencing the loss of capacity provides a significant advantage in the development of an accurate lifetime model. In this paper, the influence of the DoD factor was quantified using results of the cycling test conditions with the same temperature, mid-SoC, charge, and discharge rate. The depth of cycling within a defined DoD range may affect battery life heavily at different temperatures along with other conditions. With the impact of deep and shallow cycling, it was reported that fast degradation was found [70] and the same trend is also found in this paper for the battery cells under study, and this might be attributed to cathode ions response to a higher DoD level. Fig. 2 shows the effect of deep and shallow cycling on battery life degradation at three different temperatures and the results presented in this paper are similar to previous studies [69,70].

At room temperature of 25 °C, the DoD influence observed in Fig. 2(a) is clearly showing the relation with increasing RCD corresponding to high DoD cycling, which is coherent with the study reported in [71]. The lower trend of the 60% DoD cycling capacity drop may refer to the cell data of which one drops out from calculation after 2600 FEC. It is also observed that the cell degrades more quickly under the condition of 60% DoD than that of 80% DoD which is unusual to happen. However, it is clear that the data collected at 80% DoD was incomplete due to early failure of the cell resulted from short circuit, but after 2500 FECs it can be seen that the graph at 80% DoD started an upward trend showing that the RCD can be greater than 60% DoD if complete data was available. Fig. 2(b) depicts the number of full equivalent cycles at 35 °C where, likewise, lower DoD cycling degradation is half the number compared to the same number of cycles performed at 100% DoD. Besides, Fig. 2(c) shows the DoD dependency at 45 °C where higher DoD cycling tends to reach the EoL criterion of 80% of remaining useful capacity sooner than lower DoD cycling [12,72]. It has been found that the cycle life of this battery cell doubled if cycled at 60% DoD compared to 100% DoD. Furthermore, lower DoD cycling can extend the life as the figure suggests. The 20% DoD cycling has completed 2200 FEC with 15% capacity loss meaning longer cycle life.

Furthermore, the temperature impact on loss of capacity of NMC cells is observed when cycled with full DoD range continuously. They have degraded similarly at three temperatures of 10 °C, 25 °C, and 45 °C until 400 FEC (Fig. 3 (a)) but have parted their way afterward. However, 35 °C seems to be a better temperature for cycling completing significantly more FECs compared to room temperature cycling. It is also observed that the cells were found to be reached to their EoL faster at 100% DoD and higher temperature of 45 °C compared to 25 °C and 10 °C. The result found in this research is found to be coherent with the result of previous literature [33,73,74]. Fig. 3 in general shows the cell's good cycling capability at low temperatures comparatively.

In Fig. 3(b) and (c), the temperature dependency is shown for the mid and lower DoD range along with the mid-SoC point. At the mid-DoD range, cycling RCD at 45 °C did increase significantly than the same at room temperature which once again shows the vulnerability of battery life at high temperature. However, at low DoD cycling, high-temperature cycling RCDs show better performance with an upward trend which is almost similar to the previous studies reported in [33,73,75]. Furthermore, from Fig. 3(b), it is shown that the capacity recovered after 2500 cycles for 25 °C, such phenomena will rarely be observed at lower DoD cycling conditions resulted from side reactions of electrochemical effects where the cell may get an interphase formation and therefore one part of the electrolyte might be consumed, which results capacity gain. The other possible reason could be, increased electrode surface area with cracking of the electrodes and the anode overhang resulting the capacity recovery [31,40,42]. From Fig. 3(b) and (c), the cell capacity degrades quickly at 45 °C under 60% DoD, 50% mid-SoC, 1C/1C cycling, but slowly under 20% DoD, 80% mid-SoC, 1C/1C cycling condition. This is because, for different Li-ion battery chemistries, deep cycles cause stronger degradation than shallow cycle depths [71]. Therefore, the results in Fig. 3(b) and (c) supported this assumption, where at given temperature conditions, the cells cycled at DoD-levels of 60% and greater are commonly tended to reach their EoL faster than those at smaller cycle depths.

The charge-discharge cycling rate influence can significantly contribute to li-ion battery capacity degradation. Application of large charge-discharge C-rate resulted in faster capacity degradation and Ri increment [71]. Fig. 4 (a) shows the charge and discharge rate impact for a similar condition. C-rate is the measurement of the charge and discharge current with respect to its nominal capacity. Compared to normal 1C charge-discharge cycling, a 3C charge cycling at the same condition degraded much faster which is in line with the report presented by [76] resulting in the lithium plating effect. On the other hand, in the discharge direction, a 2C discharge completed the longest cycles, comparatively while a 3C discharge lost capacity in a similar way to the 3C charge cycling. For the 80% DoD cycling, similarly, the higher charge cycling degraded with a significantly higher speed while a 2C discharge cycling performed the same as the 1C discharge cycling condition. Therefore, it is proved that depth of charge and discharge cycling rate is a significant factor considered in the study of cycling aging [16,22]. From Fig. 4(a), it can be seen that, at 3C charge and discharge rate the cell degrades faster as compared to 1C charge and discharge rate which is found to be similar to each other.

Fig. 5 shows mid-SoC influence over mid and low DoD cycling RCD. Here, higher mid-SoC distinctly contributes to the visible aging. The influence of mid-SoC tested at 25 °C shows that cell degradation is faster at elevated SoC [77]. This proves that, though few studies were conducted on mid-SoC influence, it is important to consider mid-SoC as an essential influencing factor in the development of the aging model.

The result of mid-SoC influence provides an approximate of a linear capacity degradation effect for mid and low DoD ranges. The capacity degradation is found to be more visible at higher mid-SoC values as compared to lower mid-SoC values, having the same DoD where previous literature also [77] concludes with the same result.

For an efficient identification of the calendar aging contribution to the capacity degradation, its impact needs to be analyzed and well understood as the EV spends much time at rest. The storage SoC and temperature are the most significant factors for calendar aging [65,75,78], where the storage time were also accounted in this study. The results obtained during 12 months of calendar aging have been illustrated in Fig. 6 .

The calendaring RCD decreased initially meaning the capacity gain in calendaring cells. It can result from chemical activation of an extra anode surface after some charge-discharge cycles known as anode overhang of commercial cells [79,80] and instability of the SEI in graphite-based cells [65,78]. Besides, the impact of higher temperature on the calendar capacity loss was noticeable as reported in previous studies [69] and faster capacity fade is observed during storage of cells at higher SoC [80–82] which is commonly resulted because of imbalance between electrode and electrolyte interphase [11]. From the result, it has been found that higher storage temperature of 45 °C and higher storage SoC of 100% resulted in a higher capacity fade. At 20% SoC and 10 °C temperature, a sharp increase of capacity was found in the first 30 days where it is a result of higher resistance at a lower temperature. Through time the degradation becomes improved due to the cells self-discharge effect. Almost all the cells experienced capacity growth as stated in previous literatures. Furthermore, the capacity fade curves can say more after more months of storage tests to investigate calendar aging parameters dependency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lifetime model development of NMC cell
ce:section.ce:para: A large set of aging data has been analyzed and used to create a lifetime model as a function of different working conditions. A battery cell's lifetime is commonly characterized by the remaining discharge capacity and the value of the Ri increment [22]. The developed model primarily considers the cycling and calendar capacity fade models and then recombined them for estimating the overall capacity loss of the cell and updating the parameters. The operating boundaries considered during the lifetime model development are 100% to 20% DoD, 45 °C to 10 °C of temperature, 80% to 20% mid-SoC, 3C to 1C current rate, and 100% to 20% of storage SoC. The detailed representation of the lifetime model is shown in Fig. 7.

Where, T is temperature (°C), t represents storage time (days), SoC represents the storage state of charge, Mid-SoC stands for middle-state of charge, DoD stands for depth of discharge, C-rate is the charge-discharge current rate, FEC stands for full equivalent cycle, SoH stands for the state of health and Ri stands for the internal resistance.

The temperature and current driving profiles are the two basic inputs fed to the DP-ECM model. According to the application profile, when these inputs are provided to the model, it recalculates the electrical instant response of the cell and updates the DoD, SoC, SoH, capacity fade, Ri increase, and terminal voltage. In addition to the primary inputs, the capacity fade and Ri increase results of the cycling and calendar aging models are fed to the DP-ECM electro-thermal model as an input forming a feedback loop and therefore desired parameters are updated continuously. As the scope of this manuscript is limited to the lifetime model development, the DP-ECM electro-thermal model is not presented in this paper.

Prior to the analysis of the total degradation effect, the DoD, mid-SoC, temperature, and charge-discharge current rate influences were modeled (Fig. 7). For instance, to model the influence of DoD, the availability of the tested data at different temperatures was considered. Hence three temperatures of 10, 25, and 45 °C were considered while modeling the influence of the DoD effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DoD and mid-SoC influence model methodology
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of the DoD is modeled using the measured capacity points as input parameters. The DoD levels that are being considered in the modeling include 100%, 80%, 60%, 40%, 20%. All these conditions have dedicated cells subjected to each test condition. For every temperature that is either 10 °C, 25 °C, or 45 °C, the corresponding surfaces of these temperatures are used. Otherwise, the interpolated surface is used. The model basic input parameters considered for the DoD function are 100% DoD, 25 °C of temperature, and 50% mid-SoC. Illustrations of the input data late-life 2D extrapolation is shown in Fig. 8 .

For defining the RCD result relative to 0% of capacity, interpolation and extrapolation methods have been used for extending the sample data. The input data were fitted by a polynomial of respective order which corresponds to the particular input data. Then the resulting polynomials are appraised to the higher FEC equivalence. Using the analysis result of the experimental data, and with an extension of the input data, the surface fit of 3D representation was created at each temperature of the range of 25 °C to 45 °C. The surface fitting operation is performed by using the polynomial based semi-empirical formula shown in Eq. (1) [18]. The equation derived is obtained using an extended battery dataset generated with the pre-defined test matrix.

where; ai stands for constants for FEC, bj stands for DoD constants, n stands for FEC-factor order for the surface fit of the 3D equation, m stands for DoD-factor order for the surface fit of the 3D equation. The 3D surface fitting of the DoD function is presented in Fig. 9 .

The input data is fitted with 2D polynomials which on one hand fit the input data and on the other hand estimate the extended lifetime behavior of the NMC cells based on their respective cycling conditions. From Fig. 8 it is observed that by fitting the input data with a rational polynomial of an order suitable to the selected input data, the sample data was extended as required. The figure shows good coherence until 2000 FECs and then extrapolation is performed up to 4000 FECs which is similar to the result reported by [18]. By using the analysis result of experimental data found from Section 3.1, a 3D surface fit for each temperature between 10 and 45 °C was created after the input data extension and linear interpolation. For the 3D polynomial surface fitting, the extended measurement data was used as input data with orders for the polynomials that were unique for each temperature surface. The 3D-surface fitting of the DoD and mid-SoC functions are presented for reference 25 °C of temperature (Fig. 9). The detail illustrations of DoD function at 10 °C to 45 °C, and mid-SoC functions between 80% and 20% are presented in the supplementary material.

As it can be seen from Fig. 9, the surface fits the extended input data at respective temperatures. From the results, it is found that these surface fits are the best possible fits for the input measurements available while still providing a zero-value at BoL (0 FEC's). Accordingly, the best surface fit versus extended input measurements was found at 25 °C as compared to the other temperature ranges shown in the supplementary document.

In addition to the DoD influence, the mid-SoC influence was modeled using similar methodology of creating prolonged models using surface fitting. During the development of this model, the varying mid-SoC level at 25 °C was considered. Hence, the only temperature at which different mid-SoCs are tested is 25 °C. Therefore, it will be assumed that the behavior is the same for all temperatures. The model basic input parameters considered for the mid-SoC function are: 80%, 50%, and 20%. The illustrations of the surface fitting output of mid-SoC influence at 25 °C and 60% DoD are shown in Fig. 9. The mid-SoC function calculates the difference in RCD between the input stress conditions at 50% Mid-SoC and the RCD at the input Mid-SoC. The surfaces are used in the actual model and the model will check if the input DoD corresponds to a tested, and therefore modeled DoD. Then the model utilizes this particular surface, otherwise, the required surface will be calculated using interpolation. Finally, the baseline surface fitting parameters resulted from the semi-empirical fitting analysis are integrated into the lifetime model through the mathematical relations of aging factors. The mathematical representations and formulations of these factors are presented in the below section and their relationships are shown in Fig. 10 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mathematical formulation of aging factors relations
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the impact and relationships of the stress factors found from the experimental data analysis output, their influence on the cycling capacity loss can be related as indicated in Eqs. (2) to (6). The suited margins of these stress factors found from the semi-empirical fitting analysis are then introduced to the ECM based lifetime model with respective mathematical expressions. The cycling capacity loss expressed as a function of respective stress factors are shown in Eq. (2):
(2)

where; Cyccaploss stands for the loss of capacity due to cycling aging. Accordingly, the aging effect relations of the individual stress factors are expressed as follows:

As shown from Eq. (4), the SoC effect described by voltage relation is one factor slightly influencing the cycling degradation. This factor is related by two coefficients representing a linear relationship.

DoDeff stands for the depth of discharge influence and it is the factor which affects the cycling capacity degradation significantly. The relationship of the DoD effect is represented by logarithmic expression as shown in Eq. (5).

Teff stands for the influence of temperature, where its effect on the capacity loss was noticeable during aging investigation of the cell. The influence of temperature is commonly represented by exponential expression, which was experienced in other studies [21,25] as well.

The coefficients a1 to a6 represent the correlation factor values and need to be defined separately for each battery type. The effects illustrated in the above equations are then fed to the model to tune the cell capacity fade concerning the cycling aging factors. The rate of capacity loss of the model is estimated according to the capacity fade of 43 Ah NMC cell test results under standard baseline charge and discharge cycle data of 1C rate, 25 °C, 50% mid-SoC, and 100% DoD. The capacity fade found at this instant is considered as the baseline loss. For illustrations of the relationship between aging factors and respective correlation rates, the factors in relation to the baseline values were used as shown in Fig. 10. The curves were correlated with the base case values according to the experimental data ratio and semi-empirical fitting output of the cell capacity rate.

The relationship between each aging factor used in the lifetime model is shown in Fig. 10, where the influence of various stress factors is presented in reference to the baseline value. The values of all coefficients (a1 to a6) presented from Eqs. (3) to (6), are provided in line with the curves, where these values are fed to the lifetime model developed in Matlab/Simulink environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Input data analysis and mathematical representation
ce:section.ce:para:
The second aging effect, which causes the cell capacity degradation and internal resistance increase is calendar aging. As it can be noticed from the calendar aging results presented in Section 3.1.2, among the six storage conditions, in some cases the capacity degradation never dips below 100% SoH. This is the case for the following calendar aging test conditions:
•
10 °C:
o
20% storage SoC
•
25 °C:
o
20% storage SoC.

Besides, for the cases of (10 °C, 25 °C, 45 °C and 100% storage SoC), a capacity fade of 0.44, 0.89, and 2.7% respectively were obtained and also for the case of (45 °C and 20% storage SoC), a capacity fade of 0.4% was obtained during the same period. This behavior of the calendar aging makes the capacity fade extrapolation a difficult thing to do, as it cannot be forecasted the accurate behavior until further testing is done. To achieve more accuracy to the estimations, the surface fitting is accompanied by the Arrhenius equation for the temperature influence identification, which in turn result in the electrochemical systems side reactions exponential increase with increasing of temperature emphasizing the calendar aging [65,69,74,83]. This relationship is provided in Eq. (7) as follows [18,22]:
(7)

where; A is the loss of capacity (%), a is the pre-exponential factor, b is a fraction of activation energy (J mol−1) and T is the temperature (K). While estimating the capacity degradation effect of calendar aging and hence the lifetime of the cell, the storage SoC and storage time are also considered to account for their contribution to the capacity fade. The capacity loss because of calendaring expressed as SoC, time and temperature effects can be expressed as:
(8)

Due to the limited duration of the test campaign, the output data extracted from calendar aging test results did not show significant capacity degradation. Therefore, in addition to extracted dataset, some of the correlation factor values were estimated in reference to the previous research outputs which have been found from the similar type of battery cell and testing conditions. The degradation of capacity because of calendaring is expressed in terms of the respective stress factors and the correlation factors [22] as shown in Eq. (9), and these parameters are introduced to the lifetime model mathematically.

where; β1 to β3 is the correlation parameters relating the SoC effect, T is the temperature (°C), V is voltage effect (V) and t is time duration (days) to store the cells. For the determination of the remaining capacity of the cell, the mathematical representation of the capacity degradation resulted from cycling and calendar aging is subtracted from BoL capacity of the cell (considered as 100%) is provided as follows [84].
(10)

where, Capremaining is the total remaining capacity of the cell which is computed using Eq. (10), by categorizing the respective variable parameters of the dedicated application profile (driving cycle profile). For facilitating the calculations for months of duration, the performance parameters were taken as constant. For example, a real vehicle application profile utilized in this research is the WLTC at 90% SoC. The lifetime result of the cell related to the calendar capacity fade is presented in Fig. 11 . Besides, according to the experimental data output, the capacity fade as a function of SoH and Ri increase were related in terms of correlation factors related to outputs of the model. Hence, the relation between the resulting SoH and Ri increase has been related as:
(11)

where; γ1 to γ3 are coefficients used for regression analysis of the second order polynomial representation. The relationship and correlation factors have been illustrated in Fig. 12 .
The calendar lifetime of the battery cells at different storage SoC (100% and 20%), and temperatures of (10 °C, 25 °C, 45 °C) has been evaluated and presented in Fig. 11.

Fig. 11 shows that, the lifetime result of the cell gets higher at lower SoC and low temperature conditions. On the other hand, the least lifetime results are found at very high SoC and high temperature ranges [21]. At lower range of SoC and 25 °C of temperature, an average lifetime value is resulted. In addition to the lifetime illustrations, the relationship between the SoH and internal resistance increase of NMC cells is illustrated below.

As it can be seen from Fig. 12, the difference in SoC, DoD and temperature values significantly affects the capacity degradation and hence SoH of the battery cell. The SoH decrease was noticeable at 50% SoC and 100% DoD, with temperature values of 10 °C, 25 °C and 45 °C, and shows that the cell is reached to its EoL. The effect of increased DoD and also low and higher temperature impacts are also observed from Fig. 12, where similar result is experienced in previous studies [21,72]. On the other hand, SoH value becomes higher at 80% SoC, 20% DoD, with temperature values of 25 °C and 35 °C. On the other hand, at these conditions, the Ri values are relatively smaller.

In this section, the validation and therefore the accuracy of the developed lifetime model are presented. This is achieved by performing the relative mean error difference of the measured and simulated capacity fade results. The results of this comparison are presented in the following subsections with validation tests.

To prove the model rationality, cycling aging tests were performed as a validation test where the WLTC profile was chosen for validation purposes. The single WLTC profile performed on the cells consists of two urban plus two suburban sets (900 s each) making it an hour-long profile of 3600 s. Using WLTC profile at SoC of 90% and two scenarios of temperatures at 25 °C and 45 °C, continuous cycling was conducted for validation purposes. The detail of the validation profile is presented in the supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the model with WLTC profile
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 13 shows the validation result of the cycling aging model with test data and model simulation results. The model validation was considered for one temperature condition as sufficient data have been collected at 25 °C as compared to the cycling data available at 45 °C of temperature. The simulation result is found to be coherent with the measured capacity fade result throughout the WLTC cycles.

The small variation in degradation rate was observed with a sudden drop and increment of cell capacity which might be resulted due to the difference in storage time of driving profiles of the cells electrochemical phenomena. The simulated curve steadily followed the same path of the measurement curve within the total of 3060 WLTC cycles. Moreover, the upward lift of the RCD observed at the beginning is resulted due to the dynamic nature of Li-ion cells which causes lithium inventory. The comparison between the total measured and simulated capacity degradation results of cycling aging with respective RMSE values is presented in Table 3 . The RMSE values prove that the difference in capacity degradation rate between the measured and simulated RCD values is small, which shows that the accuracy of the model is found to be promising.

From Table 3, the RMSE value of 2% is attained and therefore it is proved that the model accuracy is acceptable. The combined aging model validation has been presented in Fig. 14 and the result shows that the total simulated and total measured capacity degradation outputs are coherent throughout the total WLTC cycles.

From Fig. 14 it is realized that a precise agreement between the total simulated WLTC curve and the measured result is achieved. The total simulated curve steadily followed the same path as the total measurement curve throughout 3060 WLTC cycles. The resulting small variation is possibly caused by the short testing duration dedicated to calendar aging which resulted in small test data. From the result, it is found that, as compared to the measured data points, the simulation result provided a RMSE of 2% which is found to be promising compared to previous results reported in [16–18].

In this research, a new lifetime model, capable of estimating the capacity fade and Ri increase values has been developed. The model was parameterized using a large dataset resulted from the cycling aging characterization tests generated for more than two years and calendar aging test conducted for one year. During the model development, DoD, Mid-SoC, C-rate, FEC and temperature factors were considered for the cycling aging. For the calendar aging model, the storage SoC, temperature factors and storage time were used. From the experimental data analysis result, the DoD, Temperature and C-rate factors resulted with significant capacity degradation of the cell and the Mid-SoC shows a slight effect. The WLTC driving profile has been used for validation of the lifetime model and it is found that the model showed reasonable accuracy (RMSE of 2%) at temperature of 25 °C. The conditions used for the result analysis include inputs of: 20% to 100% of DoD; 80%, 50% and 20% of mid-SoC; C-rate value of 1C to 3C and temperature: from 10 °C to 45 °C.

These conditions were chosen according to the cycling and calendar aging test matrix to validate the model precision with realistic testing conditions. In this study, new knowledge is gained with the development of a flexible lifetime model which can be beneficial for car manufacturers using big NMC-based cells and hence can utilize the model to their specific operating conditions to estimate the available usable capacity and lifetime of the battery cells. Moreover, the findings of the aging investigation can be used to analyze different drive profiles in terms of aging behavior.

Finally, the validation of the calendar aging modeling can be further performed in detail by considering additional test data, and therefore significant change can be observed on the calendar capacity degradation. Therefore, the performance and validation of the overall lifetime model can also be further evaluated with wide range of test results. Besides, the aging study can further be continued using postmortem analysis for identification of the cause and effects of electrochemical processes on materials of the cell. The second life study of end-of-life NMC-based cells can also be investigated further for their feasibility in different stationary application scenarios.
